query: fed rate hikes 2022 inflation stock market

videos.title: How A Fed Rate Hike Affects Your Investment Portfolio | Money Mind | Inflation
videos.text: inflation the economic bogeyman of 2022 has sent central banks scrambling to pull out their toolkits and control runaway prices in the u.s the consumer price index has hit its highest level in four decades in singapore overall inflation in december edged up to four percent on a year-on-year basis a nearly eight-year high it's the same picture across most of asia europe and the us economists polled by reuters expect price increases of anywhere between 0.5 to 5 in these regions all this is putting pressure on central banks to tighten the screws and end the era of easy money leading the charge the u.s federal reserve it has all but confirmed a rate hike next month some predict as many as seven hikes this year alone if you look at you know the cmes fed funds futures kind of a probability calculator it's showing about three times more likely that they go 50 basis points at the march meeting than 25 basis points the bank of england is poised to hike rates four times this year europe and australia are also headed in that direction and singapore unexpectedly tightened monetary policy last month we would expect more interest rate rises pretty much uniformly around the world now the question is to what extent will it be coordinated versus to what extent will some countries take action and others try to wait a little longer as the cost of borrowing becomes higher how should you reposition your portfolios the answer could lie in traditional sectors and the number one you know far and away uh area of relative strength has been the oil or energy sector historically into a rising interest rate environment and if you've seen over the last kind of six weeks plus uh all the big oil majors oil futures you know the etfs you know the drillers anything upstream anything downstream has significantly gone up others that do well as interest rates rise include consumer durables and emerging markets on the flip side tech and growth stocks are hit harder as they rely heavily on borrowing money already the tech heavy nasdaq is down more than seven percent so far this year we have stocks that don't have strong cash flows today we tend to call these growth stocks right we think they're going to generate a lot of cash flows in the future we think those stocks tend to be slightly more affected by changes in interest rates experts say diversification as always is key investors should also stay the course so if you have to pick just five or ten stocks you know maybe you want to reposition the portfolio now if you are holding a bunch of etfs or mutual funds which own thousands of stocks you are probably already well diversified and the impact of changing interest rates probably fairly minor for your portfolio inflation average is about 2.9 and stocks have historically averaged about 10.3 percent now again you know that's not saying this is automatically going to happen this year and every year thereafter but it's always been better to be invested in the long run one area to keep an eye on though the property space homeowners who took advantage of extraordinarily low interest rates during the pandemic may feel the pinch although experts say there are safety belts built into the system i've done some calculations for 20 year mortgage a 25 basis point increase in interest rates would lead to about a 2.4 percent increase in monthly mortgage payments now all these assumes that um you know the market has no seat belts on but in truth because there is a tdsr involved in the computation for mortgage uh you know for people signed taking on mortgage which uses a notional 3.5 mortgage rate this means that there's a large buffer in the market and while we are seeing now is still within that buffer zone it should not impact the market that much allen also notes that what's been driving demand in the last few years hasn't been lower rates but rather growing affluence the desire for bigger homes during the pandemic and shortages in public housing supplies money made from the stock market in particular big tech has also driven demand so how the market performs in 222 and 223 depends a lot on our opinion on how the tech stocks will perform in the next two years i'm not so concerned about all these growing measures because it's it's there but somehow the people have ways and means to still afford private residential properties with the pandemic still plodding on experts say volatility will still be rife for at least the next two quarters but if supply bottlenecks ease and economies open up more there's also the chance the global interest rate hike may not be as dramatic as feared they're going to be higher perhaps but not that that much higher so we think central banks will always lean on the side of supporting growth uh and they will be helped by a secular decline in inflation so so i think all the forces which have pushed down inflation over the past three decades it's hard to see them going fully into reverse you know so probably we're not going to see big big rate hikes higher rates are also not always a bad thing consumers may benefit from better savings account interest rates for central banks higher rates also give more buffer room to adjust to economic conditions

intraday price action turned green very briefly but just for a couple seconds and here is that down draft about 200 dow points a few points on the s p 500 that we saw there from those comments so march rate hike of 50 basis points definitely on the table we spoken with a number of analysts over the last few weeks and they seem to see think it's unlikely but maybe today changes the game a t-note yield we're just showing that up 10 basis points 2.03 percent have not seen these levels since 2019 in fact the middle of 2019 that'd be right in there somewhere in there and just marching higher as we speak and i also want to point out and this has to do with what the fed is or market participants are thinking the fed is going to do fed's bullard backs super size hike seeks one full point by july 1st and now markets are now pricing in a 75 chance of a 50 basis point rate height in march and that is up 50 uh percentage points from yesterday so here's what was priced into the market yesterday 25 to 50 basis points so that would be 1 25 basis point rate hike 76 percent odds of that those have gone down to 25 percent and instead the bets are on that 50 basis rate hike so we'll have to see how the markets digest this into the close today but very important to monitor this and just take a look at the sector action here we can see materials energy and financials it's a value and commodities that are doing well today in the stock market to the downside utilities and real estate not surprising that they're the biggest losers they're very interest rate sensitive tech that's also a big loser generally anytime you see a big march up in rates tech's going to suffer and then we get staples consumer staples is underperforming that's probably because of earnings now i just want to show the nasdaq 100 heat map a lot of red here we saw some green briefly a couple hours ago but no more apple is down one percent amazon almost right there with it microsoft off two it goes on but those are the marginal leaders here today we've seen some interest pick up in some of these beat down sectors but any gains being paired right now and then i do want to take a look at the transports as well and we can see delta airlines united each up two percent so the airline's not doing too bad but we are seeing some red in the transports as well i do want to take a look here and see if i just got a redesigned of this space so i'm looking for my heat maps here unfortunately there we go here's mobility so i was looking for those uber earnings uber a little bit of a disappointment there they did halt the stock because they announced that they're going to be even a profitable by the end of next year dd surging here you can see that stock up 11 the door dash lifts down 5 and 3 percent respectively guys okay jared lickery thank you so much we'll see you a little bit later on list

videos.title: Fighting the 2022 bear market as the Fed fights inflation
videos.text: [Music] i want to welcome everybody to another very special yahoo finance plus webinar we have liz young head of investment strategy over at so far she's going to join us in a minute here and the title of this webinar is fighting the 2022 bear market as the fed fights inflation we got some interesting thoughts on that um i just want to do a little housekeeping work here we're gonna do some polls we're gonna do one in about 30 seconds here you can ask questions we will try to get to as many as possible we really encourage that we want this to be interactive we want this to be educational and fun for you so let's start off with a poll here poll number two is let me get to my notes do you think the bottom in stocks is in that's the first poll so you bet i'm all in the new bull market is underway not sure but waiting in not sure and staying in cash or are you kidding we are in a bear market so let's welcome liz into the stream here liz really appreciate you stopping by and we had an interesting conversation earlier today and uh the the title of this webinar fighting the bear all right maybe it's not a bear because every bear market ends and every bull market begins with a low so are we there now well technically we're not anymore i suppose because a bear market is beyond 20 down i think we are out of that however i think there are a lot of people out there who have wanted to jump on the bull bandwagon and say it's all over everything's fine we had that really big rally earlier in the week unfortunately i don't think it's quite over yet and the reason i say that is because there is still a lot of data that we have to get through and that data is going to be data about earnings it's going to be data about the economy and in order to really feel confident that we have bottomed and the worst is behind us we need both of those things to come down to probably a more reasonable level given where we are with inflation and given the trajectory of what the federal reserve intends to do all right i want to i want to follow up on that in a second but let me just get to the poll results um well it looks like we're still trickling in here so let me just follow up on your definition of a bear market there are a lot of different opinions out there traders tend to look at things differently than economists and then you have the 20 down rule which you just said what is a bear market to you and what is a bowl well so a bear market it i mean i do use that definition you have to get beyond 20 and the reason why i think that's important is because with all of the electronic trading that goes on there are triggers at certain levels and that 20 level is certainly a trigger but here's the other reason it's important because once we start approaching it and this time was interesting because we approached that 20 percent level and we didn't come down off of it for a while i'm sorry we didn't cross over into 20 for a while so we kind of chopped around right around it however once you get there that's when the market has to decide do we deserve to be in a bear market and how bad does it have to get and here's what i would say on average when you have a bear market and i'm defining it as 20 when you have a bear market without a recession on average the drawdown has been 28 when you have a bear market with a been more than 40 percent so we're in that weird in between phase where the market's trying to decide okay is this a recession bear or is this just a bear in which case maybe we went down far enough but we're not going to know whether or not this is a recession bear until at least next week and probably even further into the year that's right and the official uh recession and you mentioned the r word the official recession is called by the national bureau of econom economic uh ember and they were so late to the game in 2008 2009 i don't think we knew the bear or the recession was officially over and for another year there um let me get to the results of this poll you bet i'm all in only five percent one out of 21. not sure but waiting in that was 67 not sure staying in cash almost 20 and are you kidding we are in a bear market that's 10 all right so we got that i want to pull up uh i want to go to another poll first and then maybe we can uh get back in and answer some questions poll number one or this is in my numbering when did you start investing trading is it less than a year one to two years three to five years five to ten or ten plus and i see things rolling in here we got some got some veterans we got some newbies and uh we always like to see a good mix so um liz i'm going to field one of the questions from uh the that was submitted when people registered for this event and it has to do with 60 40. this comes from dan long are bond's going to be an attractive option once the fed gets through a fed rate hike and do you see a 60 40 model portfolio i guess doing well because it's been terrible this year so far so i actually think bonds are an okay option right now and i think that i think i started talking about them a couple months ago as yields rose and we saw sell-off in bonds that we haven't seen in many many years that's coming on the heels of what you've heard many times a 40-year bull market in bonds which is very uncharacteristic of that asset class but i think right now because there is recession fear in the air and because i think we're getting to the point where the fed is going to have to say okay you know what it's working things are coming down inflation is slowly making its way down i don't know that yields are going that much higher from here so i think it's okay to be entering bonds here if you haven't had bonds in your portfolio and always remember when you're constructing your portfolio it's a three-legged stool okay the first leg of that stool is preservation the second leg might be income if that's something you're focused on and then the third leg is growth and you have to decide which one of those is most important to you and then rank them in order and that's how the assets that you put in your portfolio should reflect so if you're worried about preserving capital in this environment i do think that bonds can work if recession fears continue to get worse and worse because that's a time when you see things like the 10-year yield rally you see people flow back into really high quality securities bonds are one of those all right i just pulled up a long-term chart of the 10-year treasury note yield ties into what you're talking about uh yields moving inversely to price but you can see we are in a very long drowned uh down channel downtrend and we are just poking above these levels here and this was a very fast and furious move i'm just wondering um do you think about do you think about and this is very long term what it means when rates finally break this big downtrend and they trend upwards again or at least sideways at a higher level well it means a couple things i mean this happened because inflation hasn't been where it is today in 40 years and it was clear that it wasn't just a u.s phenomenon it was a global phenomenon and everybody around the globe is fighting inflation which then means that central banks have to react and almost every central bank has a stated mandate that says they have to try at least to keep prices stable so this is something that is reflecting the idea that central banks were going to raise rates which is exactly what we're in the middle of right now the ecb raised rates by 50 basis points today canada was 100 basis points last week so we're obviously in a cycle where central banks are trying to fight inflation now the spike up in yields was a reaction to that idea and remember the fed just started doing this in march of this year i know it feels like it's been happening for a lot longer than that really we have not been in this hiking cycle for very long the next level that's going to be interesting is when the fed's the rate the fed funds rate excuse me starts to approach the yield on the 10-year treasury that tends to be a time where the market gets a little uncomfortable because if the fed raises the rate markedly above the 10-year yield that usually is not something that markets welcome that usually causes volatility in the stock market right now we're not there yet we've got a fed funds rate that's an upper bound of 175 but next week could very quickly take that upper bound to 2.5 so when you have a treasury that's hovering around let's say 2.93 if we go up another 75 basis points from the fed next week we're in the ballpark where the fed funds rate is right around that 10-year treasury rate and then the september meeting will be the one where if it stayed steady the fed funds rate might go above the 10-year treasury rate so i think after next week's meeting this is going to be a really interesting stock bond mix to watch yeah and let me just ask you about the dollar here because that kind of ties into rates and i'm going to let me see if i can share a chart of the dixie now this is u.s dollar index it's heavily weighted to europe europe uh euro and also the pound but uh this goes back to let's see 2017 i'm going to put some more history in here we haven't seen levels in this area since way back in 2002 so we're talking about 20 years another two decade or as you've been noting um in your uh twitter feed here the dollar has puts a lot of headwinds on a lot of different markets just wondering what your thoughts on the dollar are is the move like that you move is is the move i guess done for now and what do you think if the dollar heads higher uh i don't know that it's going to get that much higher it could um but i will say when so always remember currency is a relative game right so this particular index looks like an absolute index but it's always a relative game meaning it's this currency compared to that currency so it's always the strength compared to another currency it could go higher from here and here's the thing a strong dollar is usually a headwind to multinational corporations because they get a lot of their international revenue sometimes the mix can be 60 40 even so when you look at a lot of corporations getting international revenue the dollar is usually a headwind to that it's always earth i'm sorry it's also been a headwind to gdp this year so if you look at what happened in the first quarter we had a negative gdp print because trade was a drag well trade was a drag for a couple different reasons but one of them that was a big reason was a strong dollar how does that happen i want to make sure people understand this when the dollar is strong first of all when we export goods that's positive for our economy when we import goods it counts as a negative for our economy when the dollar is strong our exports are more expensive to other countries so they buy less of them we were importing much more than we were exporting in the first quarter which is why trade became such a drag on the gdp number the dollar has stayed strong so it's probably going to be another drag maybe not as big of a drag but it's still a drag it's also again a headwind for those multinational corporations now the question was do we think it is going to keep going up do we think it falls from here i think it at least stays strong i don't know how much further it could go up the reason i think it at least stays strong is because even though we have our own set of problems in the united states we are still stronger as an economy than a lot of other nations around the globe in which case you see capital flow here more than you see it flow towards something like europe for example so i think the dollar strength is here for a while right i want to get to the results of our last poll that's when did you start investing trading uh one person said less than a year 13 percent said one to two years and also thirteen percent two to five five to ten twenty two percent ten percent are ten years plus almost fifty percent there and we're going yes we got some veterans in the room uh we're going to uh go to poll number four here what is the best recession indicator forecaster is it u.s treasury two-year 10-year spread is it two months of negative gdp us treasury three-month tenure spread or life is a box of chocolates you never know what you're gonna get we'll keep that up for a minute and i wanna get to uh another question here from the audience and again we highly encourage you to submit your questions here so that we can take them in real time um do you think tech stocks will regain the amount of losses it has lost recently for example upstart aura um i think this is a question a lot of investors have um because we've seen some of these stocks like peloton get cut 90 and i like to remind people that stock goes down 90 has to go up 900 percent in order to get back to break even that takes a while and a lot of times it doesn't ever happen uh your thoughts on this list yeah so i can't talk about individual securities i have a feeling somebody wanted to ask me about sofi but i cannot talk about that so here's what i would say there has been a lot of what i would call multiple contraction in those tech stocks now if you're asking do i think the entire tech sector can get back to where it was no at least not anytime soon because the reality is that we're still in a situation with high inflation and with tighter monetary policy which means higher discount rates and a lot of what allowed tech stocks to get to that level before was a function of having really low discount rates so as long as those rates stay high we have a headwind for tech or at least a sort of a ceiling on those high growth stocks now what i talked about a little bit before that the tenure could still come down from here if there's recession fear in the economy that is a tailwind typically for those growth stocks so you're seeing i think today still i haven't checked it in the last 45 minutes or so but the nasdaq has been doing better than the other two indices because the 10-year yield had come down so if the 10-year continues to come down or rates overall continue to come down you should see strength in the nasdaq relative to some of the other indices uh that aren't quite as tech-heavy all right we got the results of the poll the u.s so the question was what's the best recession indicator forecaster uh is it is it the two-year tenure spread 30 percent said that two months of negative gdp that's 30 u.s treasury three months 10-year spread about 25 and life is box of chocolates reference to forrest gump excuse me for the noise thirty percent so uh we talked about this a little bit before and i think we covered that fairly well i want to do a thought experiment here what we were talking about at the well we talked about this earlier um people have been waiting for the fed to pivot i say they're waiting for the fed to blink and i think you kind of agree with maybe with that terminology or a different word but we're not going to get any any big movement or from the fred anytime soon because we need more cpi prints to come and we need more economic data uh and we just need a lot more in general i'm just wondering what your thoughts are when the fed eventually starts to take off the breaks so i want to talk about the poll first too okay uh the recession indicator so two consecutive quarters of negative gdp confirms a recession but it doesn't and when you look at how do you forecast a recession now the yield curve the twos tens yield spread is a decent forecaster but the problem is defining what really counts as an inversion is a different answer depending on who you ask i think you know earlier in the year we had a brief and shallow inversion and then we had another one that was like a whisker of an inversion now this one is a little bit more notable so we're inverted by over 20 basis points it's lasted a couple weeks that's probably more like a true inversion i think actually a better forecaster or indicator to watch is what's called the near-term forward spread and what that is and this is a little complicated but what that is is the current three-month treasury yield compared to the expectation of the three-month treasury yield in 18 months okay so why does that matter it matters because what it's supposed to indicate is when the fed will have to cut rates so if the three month yield in 18 months is lower than it is today that would indicate that sometime between now and 18 months from now the fed had to cut rates likely because of a recession or some sort of really bad softening and economic data that spread is not inverted at this point it's it's tight but it's not inverted and it's it's tightened by 200 basis points since april but it's not inverted yet but i would keep an eye on that um as far as you know what happens in the recession and i'm forgetting the rest of the question what was the rest of the photos so what happens what do you need to see from the fed in order to say okay they've they've they're pivoting now or they're blinking now yeah okay also let's define what the word pivot means to me a pivot means that they start moving in the other direction right i don't know anybody who if you've played basketball when you pivot you change directions my dad was a basketball coach for 36 years so i've done a lot of pivoting but when you pivot you change directions right so to me a pivot is when they stop hiking whether that's a pause or a cut that's a pivot they could perhaps reduce the size of the hike i don't see that as a pivot i see that as a slowing in their policy tightening right now right now the market is expecting them to reduce the size of their hikes in september so it's expecting 75 basis points next week 50 basis points in september i don't think that's a pivot but i do think that would be met by perhaps some positivity in the market only if it's coupled by three consecutive months of inflation month-over-month inflation coming down that unfortunately we won't have possibly until october so if you think about we're going to get the july data that could be the first month and then the august data and then the september data that would be three months in a row but we won't find out about the september data until october so there's some time before we're going to say okay you know what they stopped hiking by as much and they did it because inflation is coming down it's working if on the other hand they stop hiking by as much because the economy has gotten notably worse that may not be met by such positivity in the market right that would scare the market if we know that they're slowing down because they say something like you know what labor market we're not feeling as comfortable anymore so we're gonna slow this down it's all about the messaging got it uh we're gonna shift gears here i think we got poll six let's do that one next what is the most important important part of investing or trading for you there's no wrong answers here just kind of a feeling risk management having a trading plan cash preservation the fed is in print mode or i'm a fly by the seat of my pants kind of investor we'll get to that in a second um so here's my pivot we want to talk about oil we talked about the dollar and uh this kind of relates to that what is going on with oil by tristan what's a lot with oil what isn't going on with oil is kind of how i feel right now um i guess i'll start with the punch line i am not somebody who's a big energy bull right now not because i don't think it's going to stay high i do think that prices will stay elevated unless we go into a recession but it it's because i think we saw the big spike already and now we're sort of in this range where we're waiting to find out whether or not there's going to be a demand destruction or a lowering of demand which would affect the price of oil and because it's so affected by some things that are completely out of our control and completely difficult to forecast and foresee such as a war between russia ukraine and any statement that comes out of the middle east or opec about the supply of oil or who knows what'll come out of the war in russia and ukraine that could de-escalate tomorrow for all we know the thing about oil and a lot of commodities especially the ones that are so affected by supply and demand is that the chart when you look at it after a little while when it changes direction it can look like blunt force trauma and you as an investor cannot catch that on that downtrend it just happens so fast that you can't possibly catch it and protect yourself so for me in this environment because i think we're getting to a point where you can wade back in to some of the sectors that you like and some of the asset classes that you like and i think you're going to get a lower chance to do it the energy sector is not a place that i'm willing to put a ton of my capital in and say okay i hope it works out i think there's just too much risk and it's not worth it at this point i think it's worth remembering that wti all-time high was just short of 150 dollars in 2007 2008 and towards the end of 2008 into early 2009 it crashed down to 30 dollars a barrel while the fud while the fed was cutting interest rates so i i want to get to the results of our last poll what's the most important part of trading for you risk management and having a trading plan where the two biggest answers each got 47 i'm i'm happy because i've asked this before and yeah the answers were different cash preservation of five percent fed in print mode nobody i'm a fly by the seat of my pants kind of investor two of you said that um let's talk about having a trading plan and risk management and the investors uh watching their own backs right now because most people have had a drubbing this year liz yeah okay here's what i would say look if you're a fly by the city or pants investor you probably get it right almost as often as people who have planned it really well here's the point of planning the point of planning is to try to remove the emotion from the equation and if you have a solid plan in place for example if you have a plan let's say for the next 60 days that you want to start averaging back into the market that plan might look something like okay i've got this chunk of money let's call it a thousand dollars just to keep it easy i've got a thousand dollars i want to put it into the market by the end of september right that's plan number one then the next plan is i'm gonna do that by investing about a hundred dollars every monday until it's gone right that's an okay plan because then you don't have to try to figure out oh gosh should i wait until tomorrow should i do it today there's bad news on whatever stock should i should i wait until it goes down further because then you're second guessing yourself you're letting emotion get in the way and you're trying not to make any mistakes at all when in reality we're all going to make mistakes and you probably can't call the bottom on every single stock and every single thing that you're doing and certainly not on the market as a whole so planning and risk management takes that emotional piece out of it when you're making investing decisions based on that sort of fomo or excessive fear you're likely to make more mistakes now fly by the seat of my pants i think that's sometimes it's okay from a timing perspective to be frank because nobody can predict it the only thing i would say is make sure that you're still diversifying your portfolio so you could be fly by the seat of your pants and really really like tech stocks because that's what you work in and that's what you're familiar with and you just like the names you use all the products i totally get that but make sure that you have exposure to other things that might sound maybe a little boring to you that might sound like you know something that only old people would buy i don't know whatever the case make sure make sure that you are diversifying it still great um here's a question from joshua west it's related what education would you prioritize a novice seeking preparation of the forecasted market so education really big i like your answer just now so go for it please uh so ed i'm assuming we mean education of if you're an investor right now and you're kind of learning as you go we're not talking about going back to school and getting a degree um i would recommend i mean the only real way to learn is by doing but i would also recommend on a daily basis especially in earnings season this is really important watch the reports of companies that you understand and try to figure out what's under the surface of them so let's say a company missed on earnings why did they miss why did they say that they missed did they have an inventory problem for example okay and i'm just trying to take you through an example so that you understand how the education piece goes so let's take um i won't say specific names let's take a large retailer that perhaps would have reported last quarter that they had an inventory issue okay and that inventory issue was a drag on their earnings i would go back and listen to why that inventory issue occurred you would find out the inventory issue occurred because they miscalculated what the consumer was going to buy how did they miscalculate it they miscalculated it because the consumer changed their mind right so what happened was consumers started to spend money on other stuff and here's what happens then you get kind of down this rabbit hole okay then you start looking into consumer spending and a lot of this data is absolutely publicly available you can find consumer spending data on the bureau of labor statistics you can find it on the bureau of economic analysis all of this is publicly available on the internet you can download the data yourself the whole thing then you start to dig into okay how did consumer spending change and what i what i would say about educating yourself is try to take the thought all the way through and figure out at the end all right what was the macro environment over that period how did it affect the corporation and that's what you find out from earnings and then last how did the market react to it and that's a that's a good formula so you take macro corporation market that's how you start to understand how the market reacts in different scenarios why the market reacts in different scenarios and you'll feel like the next time something happens or you get a bit of news that's a surprise you'll feel a little better prepared to either trade on it or not trade on it right or figure out maybe what the opportunity is that it presents tomorrow liz that's really great advice when are you writing your first book or next book i couldn't find any on amazon i don't have time right now but someday someday it's on the bucket list to write a book that was that was clever because there were two of those that happened in one day and those were huge surprises that you just don't see um let's stick with let's stick with earning actually we got to do poll three um if we can pull that up i think the greatest risk to stocks right now is what recession fed fighting inflation strong u.s dollar russia ukraine coveted lockdowns obviously no no correct answer here um but i want to follow up on earnings we chatted about this too a lot of people saying a lot of analysts saying well the eps estimates those those haven't come down yet and we're in the thick of it or we're getting into the thick of it what are you seeing and what are you expecting yeah so the rubber really hits the road next week um next week alone i think there's 168 s p components reporting it's more than 33 of the entire index so by the end of next week we're going to have a really good gauge of where earnings are this season so far companies are still beating now look they always beat right unless we're in the middle of some kind of earnings contraction companies usually beat that's just how this works i don't know why but they're beating by about four percent so we call it a surprise right so positive surprise is about four percent i don't know i don't have that factored in for whatever has happened today but four percent on average that number is about nine percent or ten percent okay so we're still beating but beating by a lower percent than average what i think we might hear next week and and these are companies across a lot of different sectors we're going to hear from a number of consumer companies we're going to hear from transportation companies and what i mean by that like ups right we're going to hear from from companies like that we'll hear from some industrials some materials companies tech companies and the ones that are goods sensitive meaning the ones that make stuff the ones that make widgets and sell it to people are the ones where you're probably going to hear about inflation having squeezed their profit margins and that they had to absorb it instead of pass it all along to customers and we're going to hear everybody's outlook for the rest of the year and maybe into 2023. so if earnings are going to come down if earnings estimates are going to come down they're going to really start coming down next week and then the week after so that's definitely something to watch it's another reason why i keep saying let's wait through the end of july because we've got so much happening next week it could be a real whipsaw week we've got so many earnings we've got a fed meeting and we've got gdp let's go over the results to this poll uh greatest risk to stocks right now recession got the biggest percentage that's 50 half of you fed fighting inflation 22 strong us dollars six percent russia ukraine 17 and coped lockdowns six percent so recession still top of mind here i want to remind you guys ask questions include and thank you for the compliment uh this is why guy if you got a question for us we'll be happy to take it here and speaking of questions let's get to one right now this kind of gets back to what we were talking about investor education um let's see i'm sorry i lost my place here as someone who was learning to invest in turbulent times i would like to know what tips you have for learning and improvement that is by vinneyaka thompson liz well i mean look i say this a lot to younger investors especially the first bear market you go through as an investor will shape you for the rest of your investing life now it doesn't mean that you can never change your mind that you can't learn more but it will shape you and you'll remember it you'll remember how much you lost you'll remember what caused it you'll know what the environment was the only real way to learn through that is to keep doing it i think the worst thing you can do is pull all your money out put it under the mattress and say i'm not doing it again until everything is better because if you look at a long-term chart of the s p and you look at even just 10-year periods there is not a 10-year period where it goes down maybe it's flat the worst that's happened i think is that it's been flat but generally speaking it still goes up so over the long term still goes up that said you can read books there are a lot of good investing books out there it's going to depend on what you're into and what how how deep you really want to dig there are a lot of really classic ones out there so i would do some searches on maybe the classic investing books um i'm thinking of things like the intelligent investor right things like that but some of those are pretty deep pretty granular and could be too much for somebody who's doing this more for sports than than really trying to do it academically um but you can also look at books like the psychology of money right things that are talking about behavioral investing and what that really means you can you can look at books by uh different financial advisors and really how they think about constructing a portfolio how they think about a timeline and again you have to do this every day you have to be looking at it consistently you have to be reading the news you have to be understanding how the news has affected the market and over time it starts to really click so it is something that's a discipline it is something that you have to just continue exposing yourself to i i think that's great i'll add a little bit here um trading is a business and i think people should treat it as one if you're not a passive investor because the competition is hedge funds with billions of dollars and they have uh processes like you wouldn't even believe not you liz but most people wouldn't believe how much they scour in their their trading books how they review their thesis every single day there's a reason that they don't sleep um also you know you also mentioned that this is that the first bear market shapes you in and uh the bear market the last bear market before this was a covered one but it was so quick that most people blinked and it was kind of over we didn't have new investors the stemming checks weren't going out yet so a lot for a lot of people for a lot of the new people this is their first one but going back to that cobin bear market what do you think about that uh the fastest one in history and i think the fastest bowl in history as well getting back to new all-time highs well the way we go into it matters and that bear market was caused by such an abrupt shock and what we saw the response that we saw from fiscal and monetary authorities was just as abrupt right it was just as fast there was immediate and emergency rate cuts i mean we had four days and this is a side story but i was actually at the new york stock exchange i think it was the third time that we hit the circuit breaker down i was there this happened this happened last march and it was the third time that we hit circuit breaker down and i'll tell you it was eerie it was i will never forget that i will never forget when that bell rang and the market closed and we halted trading because we had gone down too far that was a very abrupt very dramatic spike and stab down right and that was a time when we bounced back out of it from a market perspective but we did not bounce back out necessarily from an economic perspective those lockdowns lasted much longer than i think any of us expected and when when we came out of them then we had an inflation problem right we had a supply chain problem so i would i would argue yes the market reaction was quick but the economic recovery that occurred afterwards has been a little bumpy and i think you know for people that are experiencing this as their first bear market it's probably felt really long too because we've sort of grinded lower over the last six to eight months depending on what index you're looking at the nasdaq started grinding lower last november so it feels like this has lasted forever but realistically bear markets do tend to last longer than you know a week and a half so this you will remember this and and when you go through your next one maybe it won't seem so bad or maybe you can set your expectations better i think that the hardest part of being a new investor and a new bear market investor is to not know when it might be over and not know what's normal so to speak could i put it better myself it's time for me to do a little show here on yahoo finance plus you guys are all members some of you might have gotten the uh free trial so uh apologies if i go over something too basic for you but here is our dashboard um welcome jared and i put in a little um watch list this was a few years ago you can see uh underperforming the market by quite a bit probably because it's not diversified if you take a look right here diversification 51 of the stocks are exposed to the others sector and if we click through here we can see some uh interesting stats and helpful things at the top of the screen here's the annual performance little squid graph there risk profile moderately aggressive valuation based on the peter lynch model six are overvalued so you can see maybe you're taking on a little bit too much risk and then we have uh diversity 36 what is that 30.6 percent of the communication services sounds like a little bit much for this market um and let's take a look down here because this is going to show you where your portfolio is allocated and i think i've showed this before i'm going to skip over to um the let's see if we can get community insights so this is one of the features that has been added i believe over the last year and it keeps getting better this is based on proprietary yahoo finance data of people visiting our ticker pages that's where we get the trending tickers info that's free on our site but this goes into a lot more detail and as you know you can upload your portfolio to yahoo finance and we aggregate the data and we show you what people are buying and selling so here we have the most visited companies and we have netflix in there ibm this is going to be full of earnings names especially when we're doing those big names in earnings season like we are and this is uh helpful is too you can see what sectors are on the move or at least top of mind for investors communication services which i'm overweight in apparently consumer defensive financial services etc and then you have community conversations um here's one with dd global you can click through on here but portfolio changes that's what i just talked about this is very helpful information just to get a sense of what people are buying and selling one of the first things i do in the morning because it's part of my job is look at look at this to figure out what people are interested in so i can plan my day which is a little bit different from the average investors but um having said that let me move on to research reports this is another one of my favorite areas this uh you're going to be able to get reports on all the companies that are reporting uh you'll get analysis after the fact so here's one on amazon.com uh you can see this is after their 20 for one stock split it's going to give you all kinds of information you can download the report not going to go through it right now and then finally here i want to take people through the investment ideas let's see if i can get that up and here we go so we have investment ideas that are sent uh throughout the day and some of them are based on fundamental analysis some of them are based on technicals so here's one on jb hunt schwab netflix let's click through to netflix and you can see netflix has a bullish trend and this is a summary of the uh let's see if we can get this in a chart there we go uh this is going to tell you on the technicals uh exactly what's happening let me change this from a one month to a one day and see if we can get this annotated uh you'll have to forgive my user operator error here as i'm trying to do as i'm trying to talk at the same time so i guess i messed that up but let's get back there to research reports excuse me investment ideas and uh has it been a long day yet it's been a long year thank you liz thanks for your support here i'm here for you i appreciate that idea type let's get through let's get to um some of the fundamental ones so all these are the fundamental ones and then let's get to the technical ones for instance um here's one tgna forms a continuation diamond wedge pattern which is bullish you want to see this in the chart and it will draw it for you right there it'll give you a little explanation what it means an event description and these are automatically generated so these aren't necessarily going to be picture perfect but this is a starting point for some ideas now i want to get to a youth well i think do we yeah we got to that result and here's a question from somebody anonymous if i'm down 35 to 40 on said retailer who has inventory issues and a significant part of my portfolio we've got to flip a coin here figure this one out would you suggest selling a movie moving on to one higher potential roe trades or weight knowing i bought it at an expensive price and uh your thoughts lives okay well yeah retailer who shall not be mentioned um again this is absolutely not specific stock advice here's what here are two options i'll give you there's a thing called tax loss harvesting you could do that if you have some tax gains uh i don't know how many people have gains this year if you have some gains that you'd like to offset you can do something like that you could uh you could sell something that's down in a certain space and buy it something that you buy something that you think replaces it more or less and still participate in upside that would happen or you can replace it with something like a retail etf right but what i would actually say is that we're already seeing uh some nice optimism in the consumer discretionary names the two names that we're talking about uh really were in the staples category but just in the consumer category overall what could happen is that if fear really peaks and we find out you know what we're going into a recession or we're in one and it's going to get worse what we probably start to hear in earnings season especially from consumer discretionary is them guide down and the market does have to wade through that so it could go down further so unfortunately you're down 35 maybe it goes down more but that also then is likely a good opportunity and a good entry point so i would say i wouldn't lock in all your losses because you can't get those back once you sell out of it and really think about if you can take advantage of tax loss harvesting and that's something that you want to do maybe a little bit but i wouldn't lock it all in as a loss right now great stuff and i would add too that um you a lot of people there are people who are down 90 in some of their stocks and if you keep that in your portfolio you're going to be looking at that for a long time and it can weigh on you psychologically and i think that's an important thing to remember citigroup um is still down 90 from its 2008 high it's down 90 percent uh just writing about it earlier this morning um we got a little bit of time here so final question did you ever play basketball i went to a lot of basketball camps when my dad was a coach i spent a lot of summers at basketball camps i did actually play for a very brief period when i was a lot younger sprained my ankle once and said i never wanted to do it again but i did play softball for my entire childhood up through high school uh that was a deal i made with my dad i wouldn't play basketball but i would play softball all right that was that was not the last question i'm going to give you the field anything you want to add we got a couple minutes for our viewers as we wrap the session yeah look here's what i would say sentiment is shifting on a dime right now and we're still in a period where you could make a decision make a trade on a tuesday and feel wrong by midday on wednesday that is not an environment where you want to be incredibly active in my opinion because you're going to continue to chase your tail in a lot of different things and it gets really stressful so again i want to wait until we're past next week i want that other data to come in and i want to give the market a chance to react to it it's possible that the market likes everything that happens next week but i am okay with missing the beginning of a rally for a little while because what we really need to do as investors is avoid those huge steep drawdowns because those are the ones that we've talked about a couple times on this show those are the ones that take a lot longer to claw your way out of so i'm okay with missing a little bit on the upside if it prevents me from having an entirely large drawdown in a really quick time frame liz really looking forward to that book that you don't have time to write right now but when you do need a ghostwriter anybody here a ghostwriter uh do we have a poll for that no we don't yeah we are finally out of time here but it was a pleasure uh spending this time with you i hope everybody learned something liz young sofi head of investment strategy a big thank you to everybody who came here today participating in our polls uh and submitting questions as well we're gonna see everyone back in about one month thanks again you

videos.title: Fed Minutes Signal Possible Faster Rate Increases
videos.text: ira there was a lot of talk here at least uh in the fed minutes about the idea of balance sheet runoff and sooner uh than had been discussed before were you surprised by this well we weren't surprised we've been calling for this actually since last summer so so we think early runoff makes a lot of sense the the big thing with runoff right now is there's about over a trillion dollars of excess liquidity in the market and you can see that in the usage of the fed's reverse repurchase agreement facility which was also mentioned several times in the minutes as well and until that's kind of all run off and run down any type of balance sheet runoff will not actually be monetary tightening so it won't be any kind of you know effective quantitative quantitative tightening until that's all gone and that'll take about a year if they run off just with uh with passive runoff so just allowing maturities of treasuries and mortgage-backed securities to come off the portfolio without actually selling anything which which i think is what the fed will do because if they do ultimately actually sell anything i think that that's really going to spook the markets quite a lot yeah indeed and and interestingly we're getting notes likes of peter buckfarr over at bleakley advisory talking about the possibility of shrinking their balance sheet and raising rates at the same time powell tried that in 2018 and after a 20 stock market correction he got scared of course pivoted soon after talk to us about what's been perhaps spooking the market in the run-up to these minutes in the beginning of this year why have we seen this sudden sort of back up in yields once again when we already knew that three rate hikes was being baked in yeah well the rise in yields i don't think is quite as much to do with with the expectations of the fed's rate hikes i think it has a lot more to do with new positions so when you look at what's been going on with uh in in the futures market for example there's been a significant amount of selling of positions that look to have been covered in december so basically a lot of people have a bearish view on the treasury market but they got a little scared i think in with omicron starting in in december and then with uh uh with people worried about what was going to go on with uh qe for example in europe people covered a lot of their shorts and and kind of got flat going into year-end and i think now that we've we've passed the turn of the year where people are just resetting those positions so you know but we're right at a really important technical level right now in the 10-year yield 1.7 that was the high from last october november we break through that another seven basis points can really get there really quick up to 1.77 which was last year's yield high so um i do think that we're in a bearish trend right now and and you know it probably might not stop until we get to at least 1.77 and ira we're at a 1.6 1.6999 on the tenure so we of course love your technical analysis take us back sort of big picture a few years ago we had a federal reserve saying we're going to let inflation run hot we're really focused on this new definition of what full employment really means is this a pivot back away from that i think a little bit you know you know there was so much discussion in these minutes about inflation and inflation expectations not being as transitory as they thought and they didn't even use the word transitory they basically said it would be more persistent than they had saw it several months ago uh is the language that they used and and i think that that's one of the reasons why the markets bear flattening right now is just the idea that even if they don't hike or only hike what's being priced into the market right now um they might have to go a little further and so that's the reason why and we've been saying that that the terminal rate uh pricing was was way too low we were only pricing 1.5 fed funds rate which is basically um either either five or six interest rate hikes and we think that they're going to go at least eight maybe nine hikes um before we wind up getting to to what ultimately we think will be terminal rate a little bit above two percent uh which is still lower than the last cycle uh so you still have this very long term 40-year downtrend in rates but it's also means that it's more than the market's been pricing and that's why we think we'll continue to get bare flattening of the yield curve and and you know something that that i think will be more persistent over the next couple of months
